coredata.dc:title: A tripodal stimuli responsive self-assembly system and its application for detecting of organic amines, acids and Cu2+ ions aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jie
Wang, Bin
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent tripodal low-molecular weight gelator (LMWG) with the triaminoguanidinium cation unit as the center and triphenylamine groups modifying was designed and synthesized. The unique gelators (X) formed microfibers with different size and distribution characteristics in different solvents, which was closely crucial to the stability of gels. The self-assembly structures were performed characterization analyses by scanning electron microscope (SEM), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), rheology, transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM). It was found that the most stable gel XMG was formed in DMSO-CH3OH (1:9, v/v), which had the gelation-induced fluorescence enhancement characteristic and the fibers with small and thick structure features, uniform dispersion as well as high distribution density. In addition, XMG can be used as a multi-stimulus response fluorescent gel material to make special responses to copper ions, TEA and TFA directly and indirectly. The lowest limit of quantitation (LOQ) for TEA and TFA as well as the minimum limit of detection (LOD) for Cu 2+ were in the range of 8.24 × 10−10, 9.75 × 10−10 and 5.39 × 10−10 M respectively. The gelator possesses the potential of application in the field of detection chemical, environmental and biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Low-molecular weight gelators (LMWGs)
Stimulus-responsive fluorescent material
Colorimetric sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The responsive property towards TEA and TFA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Institute of Applied Chemistry of China West Normal University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the China West Normal University Foundation [no. 463127 and 471368 ], the Institute of Applied Chemistry of China West Normal University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Temperature-dependent fluorescence spectra of XMG (0.8%, w/v) during the gelation process (λex = 365 nm).
Partial 1H NMR spectra of X with different concentrations in DMSO‑d 6/CD3OD (1:9, v/v): 1 mg/mL (sol); 2 mg/mL (sol); 4 mg/mL (gel).
Partial 1H NMR spectra of X (4 mg/mL) in (a) DMSO‑d 6 and (b) DMSO‑d 6/CD3OD (1:9, v/v).
SEM images of X xerogels in different organic solvents: (a) CH3OH; (c) CH3CH2OH (b) XMG (DMSO-CH3OH (0.8%, w/v and 1 : 9, v/v)) and (d) XEG (DMSO-CH3CH2OH (0.8%, w/v and 1 : 9, v/v)).
XRD curves for compound X and it's xerogels formed in CH3OH and mixed solvents of CH3OH with a different organic solvent.
Strain sweep experiments for (a) XMG (0.6%, w/v) and (c) XEG (0.6%, w/v) and dynamic frequency sweep experiments for (b) XMG and (d) XEG.

(a)Time dependent pictures of XMG (0.6%, w/v) with the addition of 1 equiv. TEA, (b) pictures of the reversible responsive behaviors of XMG (0.6%, w/v) upon alternately TEA/TFA stimuli and (c) the changes of XMG fluorescence intensity alternately adding TEA/TFA.
Partial 1H NMR titration spectra of (a) X (8 mg) with the addition of (b) 5 μL TEA and (c) 10 μL TFA.
XMG in presence of different aqueous ions (a) under a UV-lamp at 365 nm (b) under daylight and (c) after the heating and cooling.
Images of X in solution (10 −5 M) with the addition of different metal ions (2 eq.) (a) under a UV-lamp at 365 nm and (b) under daylight.
Partial 1H NMR titration spectra of X with various equivalents of Cu 2+ ions in DMSO‑d 6.
Schematic representation of the possible arrangement of XMG.

head.ce:title: A tripodal stimuli responsive self-assembly system and its application for detecting of organic amines, acids and Cu2+ ions aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of China West Normal University, Nanchong, 637000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of China West Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of China West Normal University, Nanchong 637000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province of China, Nanchong, 637000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province of China, Nanchong 637000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of China West Normal University, Nanchong, 637000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of China West Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent tripodal low-molecular weight gelator (LMWG) with the triaminoguanidinium cation unit as the center and triphenylamine groups modifying was designed and synthesized. The unique gelators (X) formed microfibers with different size and distribution characteristics in different solvents, which was closely crucial to the stability of gels. The self-assembly structures were performed characterization analyses by scanning electron microscope (SEM), powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), rheology, transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM). It was found that the most stable gel XMG was formed in DMSO-CH3OH (1:9, v/v), which had the gelation-induced fluorescence enhancement characteristic and the fibers with small and thick structure features, uniform dispersion as well as high distribution density. In addition, XMG can be used as a multi-stimulus response fluorescent gel material to make special responses to copper ions, TEA and TFA directly and indirectly. The lowest limit of quantitation (LOQ) for TEA and TFA as well as the minimum limit of detection (LOD) for Cu 2+ were in the range of 8.24 × 10−10, 9.75 × 10−10 and 5.39 × 10−10 M respectively. The gelator possesses the potential of application in the field of detection chemical, environmental and biological samples.

The gel of XMG with gelation-induced fluorescence enhancement property serve as remarkable multi-stimulus response fluorescent gel materials to TEA, TFA and copper ions.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, transition metal ions are particularly easy to bring about worry because these ions will generate huge impact to the environment and human health, especially the biological activity of metal ions directly leading to physical discomfort or serious diseases [1–8]. For example, excessive intake of copper ions can lead to toxic symptoms of the liver and kidneys as well as neurophysiological problems such as Alzheimer's disease, Neuromotor disorders and Wilson's disease [9–14]. Since understanding the vital role of Cu 2+ in the life system, there always exists a great requirement for the development and utilization of Cu 2+ ions sensor [15–20]. Hitherto, a mass of fluorescent probes for Cu 2+ ions have been reported [21–25]. Nevertheless, due to the limitations of these fluorescent probes, such as low sensitivity and selectivity, working in a specific organic solvent and a narrow range of pH, the study of the new fluorescent probes for Cu 2+ has a long way to go.

Organic amines are a category of important industrial chemicals, which are widely used in pharmaceuticals and pesticides [26], but they have multifarious hazards to the environment [27]. For example, aniline is easily to volatilize and dissolve in water. Due to form a stable feature of molecular structure after entering into water, it is easy to result in a persistent environmental pollution, leading to the deterioration in the physical and chemical properties of water and sediment as well as biological population and further to deteriorate water quality [28]. On the other hand, most organic acids also have volatile properties, which will destroy environment and harm to human body [29]. For instance, the conjunctivitis, rhinitis and bronchitis may occur in the case of human contact with formic acid, and severe cases can cause acute chemical pneumonia. Although multiple detection methods of organic amines and acids have been reported recently [30,31], improving the detection methods of organic amines and acids still remains one of hot research fields in stimulus-responsive materials.

Lately, the development of multi-functional gels realized by LMWGs self-assembly has been aroused great interest due to the characteristics of LMWGs, such as simplicity, directness, regularity and low cost [32–36]. The gels are formed by multiple weak non-covalent interactions as the main driving force, such as hydrogen bonds, π-π stacking, van der Waals, etc., which endows them with sensitivity to external stimulus such as temperature, pH, solvents and guest ions [37–41]. The excellent characteristics have brought LMWGs a wide range of applications in information transmission, cell imaging, drug and protein delivery, and sensors [42–49]. Nowadays, the design and synthesis of new LMWGs become significant researches direction of medical, material and biological science.

While LMWGs is peculiarly intriguing and always in great demand, it should be noted the simpler the molecule, the weaker the non-covalent interaction between the molecules, which is undoubtedly adverse to gelation. In our study, a tripodal low-molecular weight gelator was designed by taking a triaminoguanidinium cation (L) as the center, which provides an adequate environment to generate numerous hydrogen bonds. In addition, with the booming field of stimulus-responsive gels, a simple gel-sol response behavior no longer satisfies the demands for a wider range of applications. Fluorescence groups provides valuable optical information for external response signals to effectively expand the application prospect of LMWGs in the field of stimulus-responsive materials. Hence, triphenylamine groups with excellent fluorescence property were introduced into the molecule [50,51]. Moreover, the triphenylamine group with propeller-like spatial structure act as an AIE-active unit, which efficiently avoid the impact of the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect. Finally, schiff base is always considered as a outstanding chelating ligand, therefore, specific metal ions could be identified and analyzed by combining with them quantitatively [52–54]. As shown in Scheme 1 , the X was synthesized by rationally linking 4-formyltriphenylamine and the triaminoguanidinium cation through a simple synthesis step in high yields (83%).
The prepared compound X was characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR and ESI-MS (Figs. S3–S5).

Initially, we adopted the method of “stable to inversion of a test tube” to verify whether X could be self-assembled in various solvents. As shown in Table S1, positive results only occurred in methanol and ethyl alcohol. However, the stability of the gels was relatively poor (sloshing would lead the gels to the collapse). It was worth noting the solubility of X was not high when it heated to dissolve in methanol or ethanol. In order to increase the solubility of X to improve the stability for gels, a certain amount of DMSO were added to methanol and ethanol respectively (DSMO - methanol or ethanol, 1:9, v/v). Expectantly, these mixtures could form gels XMG and XEG respectively after heating and cooling. Interestingly, the stability of XMG and XEG had been substantially improved. In this case, XMG and XEG were obtained within 1 min after stoppage of heating to the solutions. In these gels, the lowest critical gelation concentration (CGC) for XMG was 0.4% (w/v, 1 mg/mL = 1%). Moreover, it was found that the gels were stable at room temperature at least 7 months in closed test tubes and the reversible gel-sol transition was still feasible by heating-cooling method. Similarly, the addition of other solvents, such as THF, CH2Cl2 and CHCl3, to X in methanol or ethanol also showed positive results.

As shown in Fig. 1 , when the gel of XMG was heated into hot solution (T > TGel), it had a negligible fluorescence emission. However, when the temperature of the solution gradual decline to below the TGel (T < TGel), the solution converted into a stable organic gel with a strong green fluorescence under the UV lamp at 365 nm. These phenomena indicate that XMG has a typical gelation-induced fluorescence enhancement property due to the restricted rotation of single bonds for linking triphenylamine groups in the gel state [55–57].

In order to confirm the arrangement mechanism for self-assembled 3D network of XMG at the molecular level, a concentration-dependent 1H NMR of X in DMSO‑d 6/CD3OD (1:9, v/v) at room temperature were carried out (Fig. 2 ). With the increase of concentration, the signal of H1 did not show any change, but H2 and H3~6 on the benzene rings showed slight shifted upfield, indicating that the triphenylamine groups have feeble π-π interactions in the self-assembly process. In addition, the same concentration of 1H NMR studies of X in DMSO‑d 6 and DMSO‑d 6/CD3OD also provided crucial insights into the gels formation (Fig. 3 ). Compared to the 1H NMR in DMSO‑d 6, the signals of H1 and H2 in DMSO‑d 6/CD3OD had been shifted upfield by leaps and bounds, particularly the signal of H1. This occurrence illustrated a certain of intermolecular hydrogen bonds were formed between hydrogen atoms on the NH groups and nitrogen atoms on the C=N groups in the self-assembly process. Based on the above results, the self-assembly of XMG is explained in Scheme 2 .

All these results showed that the stability of gels formed by adding the high solubility solvents was higher than the one in a pure methanol or ethanol, which is due to the addition of these solvents promoting more gelators to participate in the self-assembly process. Ultimately, the intermolecular hydrogen bonds and π-π stacking interactions were enhanced to improve the stability of gels.

To explore the difference of stability between gels formed by adding a highly soluble solvent to methanol and ethanol, the rheology, TEM and AFM experiments were carried out using XMG and XEG as representatives to further understand the relationship between stability and mesoscopic size of fibers.

In the rheology experiments, Strain sweep and frequency sweep had been performed to study the mechanical strength of XMG and XEG gels (0.6%, w/v). As shown in Fig. 6 a and c, storage modulus (G ′) was larger than that of loss modulus (G ″) in the linear viscoelastic region (LVR) at low strain amplitudes, reflecting its great gel state. However, the collapse of the gels were observed for the high strain amplitudes of all the two samples (G' < G″). The gel of XMG was destroyed when the strain upon 32.81 ± 2.11%, while the XEG was broken when the strain upon applying 7.94 ± 1.31%. Fig. 6b and d revealed that G′ was greater than G″ for both gels, indicating that both of G′ and G″ were independent of the given frequency range. Interestingly, the rheological results showed the mechanical strain strength of XMG was higher than XEG.

TEM images (Fig. S8) showed the cross sections of the two xerogels. Compared with the distribution characteristics for xerogel fibers of XEG, the fibers of XMG were uniformly dispersed and had higher distribution density. Moreover, AFM experiments were performed for XMG and XEG (Fig. S9), which revealed the fibrilar nature of gels in the mesoscopic scale. The height profile (Figs. S9a and S9b (inset)) of the AFM images in the gels of XMG and XEG were observed to be 251 and 208 nm respectively. One emphatic point is that XMG formed a thicker fiber structure than that of XEG.

Combined with TEM and AFM images analysis, it was easy to find that the fibers of XMG had the characteristics of smaller and thicker size, uniform dispersion and large distribution density, when compared with the microscopic properties of XEG. This explains why the stability of XMG was higher than that of XEG in the rheology experiments. Similarly, after adding other highly soluble solvents to X in methanol or ethanol, the same results were also obtained by comparing the gels fiber size in the SEM images (Fig. S6).

Based on the above experiments, it was shown that the micro-size of gel fiber could be regulated by different mixed solvents to affect the gel physical properties. Due to the sensitivity of non-covalent interactions to external stimuli, including temperature, competitive objects and acid-base stimuli, gels typically possess stimuli-responsive behaviors. Therefore, we chose a rapid sol-gel transition and high stability gel XMG to execute the following studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The responsive property towards TEA and TFA
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we examined the response characteristics of XMG (0.6%, w/v) to acids and bases (Fig. 7 ). The gel was gradually dissolved to form a transparent solution after adding TEA (0.05 mL). Notably, the green fluorescence of XMG gradually disappeared during the gel dissolution process (Fig. 7a). However, the gel and fluorescence reappeared when TFA was added to the above system (Fig. 7b). Meanwhile, XMG showed a reversible gel-sol transition at the continuous stimulus of TEA and TFA (Fig. 7c). In the same way, the fluorescence response of X to TEA and X + TEA to TFA were investigated in DMSO-CH3OH (1:9, v/v) as shown in Fig. S10. The fluorescence emission peak at 490 nm decreased with the addition of TEA. Nevertheless, the fluorescence emission peak recovered after adding TFA to this system. Interestingly, a single TFA had no impact on the fluorescence for X solution. To further examine the response efficiency of X to TEA and X + TEA to TFA, the fluorescence titration experiments were carried out. The fluorescence titration curve of X to TEA exhibited a good linear relationship (Fig. S11). According to the 10 δ/S method, LOQ was estimated to be 8.24 × 10−10 M. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually with the addition of TFA to the X + TEA system (Fig. S12). Similarly, LOQ was estimated to be 9.75 × 10−10 M.

Meanwhile, the response characteristics for other acidic and basic compounds were investigated as shown in Fig. S13. Just like the result of adding single TFA, there never existed any changes in the fluorescence under the conditions of CH3COOH, HCl (aq) and H2SO4 (aq), indicating the strong stability of X under acidic conditions. On the contrary, the fluorescence of X disappeared under the conditions of KOH (aq) or NaOH (aq). Likewise, the fluorescence recovered after adding a acid to the system. These results showed X could not directly interact with acids, but with bases. Moreover, the bases such as TEA, KOH (aq) and NaOH (aq) were not distinguish by X.

Next, The response mechanism of XMG to TEA and XMG + TEA to TFA were studied by 1H NMR, UV–vis spectra and SEM. In the 1H NMR experiments (Fig. 8 ), when TEA was added to X, the signal of H2 obviously shifted to the upfield and N–H disappeared due to the deprotonation process. With the addition of TFA, the H1 and H2 peaks returned to their original state. In addition, the absorption peak at 395 nm had a blue shift after the addition of TEA in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S14). However, the absorption peak recovered completely after the addition of TFA. Both the SEM images of XMG and XMG + TEA + TFA were fibrous (Fig. S15). The results showed TEA and TFA could be continuously detected through the process of the fluorescence quenching and recovering as well as a reversible gel-sol phase transition.

To explore the sensing response of XMG toward aqueous ions such as K + , Fe 3+ , Mn 2+ , Na + , Ca 2+ , Hg 2+ , Mg 2+ , Cu 2+ , Cd 2+ , Co 2+ , Zn 2+ , Al 3+ , Ni 2+ and Pb 2+ , 2.0 equiv. of various metal nitrate salts were added and diffused into XMG (0.6%, w/v) respectively. Interestingly, as shown in Fig. 9 a–b, the phenomenon of a visible color change and the quenching of green fluorescence were observed for XMG only in the presence of Cu 2+ ions, while other ions didn't trigger, indicating that XMG could selectively detect Cu 2+ . Unfortunately, it took about 6 h to make a significant change in the whole gel. In order to reduce detection time, the influence of adding metal ions for gelation was investigated by the heating-cooling method. As shown in Fig. 9c, XMG adding Cu 2+ ions was observed to collapse into partial gel by natural cooling after heating and the color also turned dark green. On the contrary, the gels with adding other cations immediately formed gels again after heating, and there wasn't a distinct change in the gels. These results showed that XMG could efficiently and selectively identify Cu 2+ ion. The emphasis is that the whole stimuli-responsive process took less than 2 min. In addition, we also studied the effect of different concentrations of Cu 2+ ion aqueous solution on gelation process (Fig. S16). When the content of Cu 2+ was less than 1 × 10−3 equivalent, the gel formed again. This result meant that the minimum amount of Cu 2+ in water detected by this method was 0.42 mg/L, which is lower than the acceptable concentration of copper ion in drinking water recommended by the World Health Organization (WHO).

On the other hand, a preliminary study about recognition characteristic of X to various cations (2 equiv. of Fe 3+ , Mn 2+ , Na + , Ca 2+ , Hg 2+ , Mg 2+ , Cu 2+ , Cd 2+ , Co 2+ , Zn 2+ , Al 3+ , Ni 2+ and Pb 2+ ) was performed by fluorescence emission spectra in DMSO-CH3OH (1:9, v/v). The fluorescence emission peak at 490 nm decreased with addition of Cu 2+ and Fe 3+ respectively. Oppositely, other cations did not induce any significant changes in the fluorescence emission spectrum (Fig. S17). Additionally, a color change of X solution from transparent to yellow or light green was correspondingly observed by the naked-eye in the presence of Fe 3+ or Cu 2+ ion (Fig. 10 ).

The selective responses to Fe 3+ and Cu 2+ were monitored by the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. S18). The absorption spectrum of X have absorption peaks at 290 and 390 nm. With the addition of Fe 3+ and Cu 2+ , the two absorption peaks disappeared and new absorption bands appeared respectively. These means that Fe 3+ and Cu 2+ ions possessed a good coordination with X and formed a stable structure. However, other cations did not induce significant changes.

To evaluate the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Fe 3+ or Cu 2+ , the fluorescence titration experiments were recorded. There was a linear relationship (R2 = 0.999) between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Cu 2+ (Fig. S19b). Based on the 3 δ/s square method, LOD for Cu 2+ kept 5.47 × 10−10 M [58]. The association constant (Ka) was calculated and the slope was 2.57 (Fig. S20), manifesting that X formed a complex with Cu 2+ ions in the 1:3 relationship [59]. Regrettably, There never existed a clear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Fe 3+ . This may be related to the irregular changes of coordination relationship between Fe 3+ and X in the process of complexation.

We also studied the effect of different pH on the process of detection Cu 2+ (Fig. S21). The fluorescence intensity of X and X@Cu
2+ were investigated by properly adding HCl or NaOH solution to adjust the pH value of the sample solution. The results showed that X and X@Cu
2+ possessed stable fluorescence intensity in the pH range of 1–7, which was related to the stability of X under acidic condition. Since Cu 2+ generally exists in acidic or neutral environment, this result substantially elevate the practical value of X.

The mechanism for detection Cu 2+ ions had been precisely investigated by 1H NMR. As shown in Fig. 11 , the proton signals of H1 and H2 peaks did not disappear, but showed shifted upfield in the presence of Cu 2+ ions, manifesting the interaction for nitrogen atoms of N–H and C=N respectively with Cu 2+ ions during complex formation. Moreover, the clearly separated signals of H3~6 also became blurred gradually with the addition of Cu 2+ , which showed that X molecules assembled around Cu 2+ to form tightly aggregated complexes. With the increase of Cu 2+ concentration, larger and tighter aggregation complexes were formed. This result would contribute to the relatively rigid planar structure of triphenylamine groups to be bent, and the new aggregation complexes led to strong chelation-enhanced fluorescence quenching (CHEQ) [60]. Interestingly, when Cu 2+ exceeded 3 equiv, there was no further influence for the shape of these broad peaks in each proton signal, which confirms that X combined with Cu 2+ by the 1:3 mode complexation.

In addition, the sensing mechanism of XMG to Cu 2+ was further investigated by FT-IR, XRD and SEM experiments. At first, the SEM images (Fig. S22) showed that the fiber structure of XMG changes into a winding and overlapping clastic structure after complexing with Cu 2+ . Secondly, the all X-ray peaks of the xerogel were weakened after adding Cu 2+ (Fig. S23), which could be attributed to a certain extent destruction of the intermolecular hydrogen bonds and π-π stacking in XMG. Lastly, in the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S24), with the addition of Cu 2+ into XMG, the stretching vibration absorption N–H and =C–H had a slight shift from 3446 to 2923 cm−1 to 3444 and 2921 cm−1 respectively, In addition, the C=N signal moved from 1633 to 1621 cm−1. These results indicated Cu 2+ coordination with nitrogen atoms in the NH and CN groups.

Overall, we have developed a new type of fluorescent tripodal gel materials. Interestingly, the different of solvents affect the gelation ability. The self-assembly structures of the gels formed in different solvents were performed characterization and analysis by rheology, SEM, XRD, TEM and AFM. The most stable gel XMG was formed in DMSO-CH3OH (1:9, v/v), which has characteristics of microfibers. In addition, the compound X could sensitively and selectively detect Cu 2+ , TFA and TEA both in solution and gel state. Firstly, XMG showed the ability of rapid detection of TEA and TFA continuously. Under the stimulation of TEA and TFA, a reversible gel-sol phase transition and grass green fluorescence quenching and recovery were achieved. Meanwhile, the reversible detection of TEA and TFA could be cycled at least 10 times. Secondly, the phenomena of a visible color change and the quenching of fluorescence were observed for XMG in the presence of Cu 2+ ions. Finally, LOQ for TEA and TFA as well as LOD for Cu 2+ were in the range of 8.24 × 10−10, 9.75 × 10−10 and 5.39 × 10−10 M respectively, which is much lower than the detection limit in other recently reported aqueous media. This kind of multi-stimulus response fluorescent gel material with low cost, simple synthesis and high efficiency for the detection of multiple analytes has practical application value in the field of environmental pollution detection.

Jie Xu: Formal analysis, Data curation, conceived the idea, carried out the synthesis experiments, purification process and material property experiments, wrote the manuscript. Bin Wang: Conceived the idea and modified the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the China West Normal University Foundation [no. 463127 and 471368], the Institute of Applied Chemistry of China West Normal University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109643.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of heavy metal ions based on quantum point contacts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Rajagopalan V, Boussaad S, Tao NJ. Detection of Heavy Metal Ions Based on Quantum Point Contacts. Nano Lett 2003;3:851-855.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Testing for toxic elements: a focus on arsenic, cadmium, lead, and mercury

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Keil DE, Bergerritchie J, McMillin GA. Testing for Toxic Elements: A Focus on Arsenic, Cadmium, Lead, and Mercury, Lab. Med 2011;42:735-742.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selectively and sensitively monitoring Hg 2+ in aqueous buffer solutions with fluorescent sensors based on unnatural amino acids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lohani CR, Neupane LN, Kim JM, Lee KH. Selectively and sensitively monitoring Hg2+ in aqueous buffer solutions with fluorescent sensors based on unnatural amino acids. Sens Actuat B 2012;161:1088-1096.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pillararene-based fluorescent chemosensors: recent advances and perspectives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen JF, Lin Q, Zhang YM, Yao H, Wei TB, Pillararene-based fluorescent chemosensors: recent advances and perspectives. Chem Commun 2017;53:13296-13311.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pillararenes as macrocyclic hosts: a rising star in metal ion separation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen L, Cai Y, Feng W, Yuan L. Pillararenes as macrocyclic hosts: a rising star in metal ion separation. Chem Commun 2019;55:7883-7898.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots using Chionanthus retusus fruit extract and investigation of their suitability for metal ion sensing and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Atchudan R, Edison TNJI, Chakradhar D, Perumal S, Shim JJ, Lee YR. Facile green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots using Chionanthus retusus fruit extract and investigation of their suitability for metal ion sensing and biological applications. Sens Actuat B 2017;264:497-509.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pillar[5]arene-based multifunctional supramolecular hydrogel: multistimuli responsiveness, self-healing, fluorescence sensing, and conductivity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen JF, Lin Q, Yao H, Zhang YM, Wei TB. Pillar[5]arene-based multifunctional supramolecular hydrogel: multistimuli responsiveness, self-healing, fluorescence sensing, and conductivity. Mater Chem Front 2018;2: 999-1003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The optoelectronic nose: colorimetric and fluorometric sensor arrays

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li Z, Askim JR, Suslick KS. The Optoelectronic Nose: Colorimetric and Fluorometric Sensor Arrays. Chem Rev 2019;119:231-292.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Triple-indicator-Based multidimensional colorimetric sensing platform for heavy metal ion detections

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Idros N, Chu DP. Triple-Indicator-Based Multidimensional Colorimetric Sensing Platform for Heavy Metal Ion Detections. ACS Sens 2018;3:1756-1764.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Parameters affecting the synthesis of carbon dots for quantitation of copper ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lin YS, Lin YL, Periasamy AP, Cang JS, Chang HT. Parameters affecting the synthesis of carbon dots for quantitation of copper ions. Nanoscale Adv 2019;1:2553-2561.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmental copper sensor based on polyethylenimine-functionalized nanoporous anodic alumina interferometers

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Turn-off/on fluorescent sensors for Cu 2+ and ATP in aqueous solution based on a tetraphenylethylene derivative
